coredata.dc:title: Development of an inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassay for the detection of acrylamide using 9-xanthydrol derivatization
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Lin
Jia, Bao-Zhu
Wei, Xiao-Qun
Xiao, Zhi-Li
Wang, Hong
Sun, Yuan-Ming
Shen, Yu-Dong
Lei, Hong-Tao
Xu, Zhen-Lin
coredata.dc:description: The far-ranging occurrence of acrylamide (AA), a Group 2A carcinogen, in extensively consumed processed foodstuffs has aroused worldwide public health concern, and heightened the need for rapid and reliable detection technologies. Current immunoassays for AA are mainly based on antibodies against mercaptobenzoic acid (MBA) derivatized AA, and thus require a relatively complicated pre-analysis sample derivatization using MBA (heating > 50 °C, time>1 h), which limits the analysis efficiency. In this work, two haptens against xanthyl acrylamide (XAA) were synthesized, and a specific and high-affinity polyclonal antibody against XAA (anti-XAA pAb) was obtained. By virtue of this antibody, a fluorescence immunoassay for quantification of acrylamide with a simple sample derivatization by 9-xanthydrol (ambient temperature, 30 min) was developed via introducing the fluorescent signal of carbon dots (CDs) through the facile inner-filter eﬀ ;ect (IFE) strategy. This fluorescence immunoassay displayed a sensitive and specific response to AA with a detection limit of 0.16 μg/L, which was lower than the legal limit in drinking water (0.5 μg/L) set by WHO. Recovery test and method validation by HPLC-MS/MS demonstrated its good accuracy and reliability, suggesting that this fluorescence immunoassay can be a potentially effective tool for the supervision of AA contamination in foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acrylamide
Fluorescence immunoassay
Inner filter effect
Hapten
Antibody

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures of the IFE-based ﬂuorescent immunoassay
Feasibility of the inner filter effect-based fluorescence sensing of ALP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Project of Guangdong Provincial High School, China
Special Support Program of Guangdong Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31822039 , 31801621 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2018YFC1602903 ), the Special Support Program of Guangdong Province, China ( 2019TX05N052 ) and the Key Project of Guangdong Provincial High School, China ( 2019KJDXM002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Derivatization reaction between acrylamide and 9-xanthydrol; (B) Scheme of the synthesis of haptens XAA-295 and XAA-309.

(A) TEM image of CDs (inset: size distribution of CDs); (B) HRTEM image of CDs; (C FTIR spectrum of the CDs; (D) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs and UV–vis spectra of PNPP and PNP.

(A) UV–vis spectra of enzymatic reaction solution upon addition of serials of concentrations of ALP (0–400 U/L) with PNPP (5 mM) after 30 min incubation at 37 °C; (B) Fluorescence response of CDs + PNPP system to serials concentrations of ALP (0 –100 U/L); (C) Calibration curve for ALP sensing. The equation is: y = 7.24x+0.393, (R2 = 0.988); (D) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs before (black, 11.47 ns) and after (red, 11.42 ns) quenching by PNP.

(A) Fluorescence response of the fluorescence immunoassay to AA standards with different concentrations (0–1000 μg/L); (B) The calibration curve of the fluorescence immunoassay for AA. Inset was the linear region of calibration curve.
Schematic illustration of IFE-based fluorescence immunoassay for the determination of AA using 9-xanthydrol derivatization.
Characterization of antiserum against XAA using homologous and heterologous coating antigens.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Anti-XAA-295#1 represents the antiserum from NO.1 rabbit immunized by immunogen derived from hapten XAA-295.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Titer is defined as dilution factor of antiserum with the absorbance at 405 nm being situated at about 1.0 at coating concentration of 1.0 mg/L.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Percentage inhibition was expressed as follow: Inhibition (%)= [1-(B/B0)] × 100. B0 was mean absorbance of the wells in the absence of competitor; B was mean absorbance of the wells in the presence of certain concentration of competitor (100 ng/mL XAA).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of acrylamide in spiked samples by the fluorescence immunoassay and HPLC-MS/MS (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a. / means that the obtained result was below 0.2 μg/L, the LOD of the method.

head.ce:title: Development of an inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassay for the detection of acrylamide using 9-xanthydrol derivatization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety/Guangdong Laboratory of Lingnan Modern Agriculture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety/Guangdong Laboratory of Lingnan Modern Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety/Guangdong Laboratory of Lingnan Modern Agriculture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biology and Food Engineering, Guangdong University of Education, Guangzhou 510303, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biology and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biology and Food Engineering, Guangdong University of Education, Guangzhou 510303, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The far-ranging occurrence of acrylamide (AA), a Group 2A carcinogen, in extensively consumed processed foodstuffs has aroused worldwide public health concern, and heightened the need for rapid and reliable detection technologies. Current immunoassays for AA are mainly based on antibodies against mercaptobenzoic acid (MBA) derivatized AA, and thus require a relatively complicated pre-analysis sample derivatization using MBA (heating > 50 °C, time>1 h), which limits the analysis efficiency. In this work, two haptens against xanthyl acrylamide (XAA) were synthesized, and a specific and high-affinity polyclonal antibody against XAA (anti-XAA pAb) was obtained. By virtue of this antibody, a fluorescence immunoassay for quantification of acrylamide with a simple sample derivatization by 9-xanthydrol (ambient temperature, 30 min) was developed via introducing the fluorescent signal of carbon dots (CDs) through the facile inner-filter eﬀ ;ect (IFE) strategy. This fluorescence immunoassay displayed a sensitive and specific response to AA with a detection limit of 0.16 μg/L, which was lower than the legal limit in drinking water (0.5 μg/L) set by WHO. Recovery test and method validation by HPLC-MS/MS demonstrated its good accuracy and reliability, suggesting that this fluorescence immunoassay can be a potentially effective tool for the supervision of AA contamination in foods.

ce:section.ce:para: Acrylamide (AA) is a hazardous organic compound, which has known toxic effects on the nervous system and on fertility [1]. International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) listed it as a Group 2A carcinogen in 1994 [2]. Since its serious contamination in thermally processed starchy foodstuffs was reported in 2002 [3], significant concern has been raised. WHO has regulated the maximum residue limit of AA in drinking water should not exceed 0.5 μg/L [4]. Although no legal limit for AA in other foods has been issued, risk assessments of daily acrylamide exposure are well under way. As a result, there is drastically growing demand in monitoring AA level in foods.

Up to now, many instrumental analytical methods, such as HPLC, LC–MS/MS, GC–MS have been adopted to detect AA content in foodstuffs [5–7]. Although these methods could detect AA with good accuracy and sensitivity, they are expensive, time-consuming and require professional staff. Considering the substantial cost implications, it is impractical to use instrumental analytical methods to routinely analyze large number of samples. Antibody-antigen specific interaction-based immunoassays have proven to be effective tools for monitoring chemical residue [8–10], and become good complementary for instrumental analytical methods due to their simplicity, specificity and low-cost. To date, there have been several studies on antibody production and development of immunoassay for AA [11–17]. However, the production of a high-affinity antibody which could directly recognize AA is extremely difficult due to its small size and simple structure lacking in strong epitope groups. Therefore, reported immunoassays for AA detection are mainly based on antibodies against its derivatives, such as mercaptobenzoic acid (MBA) derivatized AA (AA-3-MBA or AA-4-MBA), and a pre-analysis sample derivatization with 3-mercaptobenzoic acid (3-MBA) or 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MBA) is required to transfer AA into the corresponding derivative [12,14,12–17]. However, the completion of the derivatization reaction between AA and MBA requires heating (>50 °C) and relative long time (>1 h), which limits the analysis efficiency of immunoassay. Thus, it is highly desired to develop an immunoassay for detection of AA using a facile and rapid derivatization procedure. Xanthydrol is a typical derivatization reagent used for identifying and quantifying the content of urea [18]. This reagent also can efficiently react with the amide group of AA to yield xanthyl acrylamide (XAA) in aqueous solution under mild conditions at ambient temperature (Fig. 1 A). Some researchers have developed 9-xanthydrol derivatization-based GC–MS for the quantification of AA in drinking water and foods, in which sample derivatization step can be completed within 30 min at room temperature [7,19,20].

In this study, two haptens, named as XAA-295 and XAA-309 (Fig. 1B), against xanthyl acrylamide (XAA) were firstly synthesized. These two haptens were coupled to keyhole limpet hemocyanin (KLH) and ovalbumin (OVA) to prepare immunogen and coating antigen, respectively. Then, a high affinity polyclonal antibody which can specifically recognize XAA was obtained by immunizing New Zealand white rabbit. As increasing numbers of fluorescent nanomaterials with exceptional optical property emerge, development of sensitivity-enhanced fluorescence immunoassay by converting a colorimetric absorbance signal into a ﬂuorescence signal via various physical and chemical properties of nanomaterials has garnered considerable interest [21–23]. The inner filter eﬀ ;ect (IFE) is a kind of non-irradiation energy transfer model in spectrofluorometry, which is resulting from the absorption of excitation or emission light of the ﬂuorophores by the absorber in the sensing system. Since the changes in absorption intensity of absorber convert into exponential variations in the fluorescence intensity of fluorescer, the IFE has proven to be an effective and potent strategy for enhancing the sensitivity of the detection system [24,25]. Li et al. prepared a kind of photoluminescent gold nanocluster of which fluorescence can be efficiently quenched by p-nitrophenol (PNP), a typical alkaline phosphatase (ALP) catalytic product, through IFE. Then, an IFE-based fluorescence immunoassay for highly sensitive determination of bromadiolone was developed by using gold nanocluster as the signal probe [25]. However, gold nanocluster is a kind of noble-metal-based nanomaterial, its cost should be considered when widely put into use. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have garnered increasing research interest in recent years because of their exceptional optical properties, ease of synthesis, and admirable biocompatibility. These merits make CDs to be a potent alternative to the traditional heavy metal-containing QDs and display great potential in biosensing and bioanalysis application [26,27]. Herein, we synthesized a kind of green-emission CDs of which fluorescence could be efficiently quenched by p-nitrophenol (PNP) through IFE as well, then using anti-XAA antibody as a recognition reagent, and CDs as a ﬂuorescent probe, an IFE-based ﬂuorescence immunoassay was developed for sensitive detection of AA in foods (Scheme 1 ).
Reagents and instrumentals are presented in Supplementary materials.
Two haptens, named XAA-295 and XAA-309, were synthesized according to the route presented in Fig. 1B.

XAA-295. 9-acrylamido-9H-xanthene-3-carboxylicacid (6). Intermediate, xanthen-9-one-3-carboxylic acid (4) was synthesized according to a previous study [28]. Briefly, 9.9 g of bromotherethphalic acid (1), 2.4 g of phenol, 5.6 g of 1,8-diazabicyclo [5.4.0] undec-7-ene (DBU), 0.2 mL of pyridine, 0.1 g of copper (0), and 0.1 g of copper(I) iodide were added to 100 mL of dimethylformamide (DMF). The reaction mixture was refluxed at 80 °C for 2 h. Then, the reaction was cooled to room temperature, and adding HCl (1 M) to it until no more precipitate yield. The resulting precipitate was filtered and washed with water and dried under vacuum to give the intermediate (3) as greenish solid. Intermediate (3) of 2.7 g was added to 100 mL of H2SO4 (80 %) and heated under reflux in an oil bath (80 °C) for 3 h. When cooled to ambient temperature, the reaction mixture was poured over 300 mL of ice water yielding gray precipitate. After filtration, the gray precipitate was washed with water, and dried to obtain expected product as black solid (4). Then, the intermediate (4, 1.2 g) was dissolved and under reflux in 50 mL EtOH. Both 0.6 g of sodium and 0.1 g of activated Zn powder were added. After refluxing for 24 h, the reaction solution was cooled to ambient temperature, and then the reaction solution was acidified by adding HCl (6 mol/L). Then, the formed precipitate was removed by filtration. Next, excess AA was added to react with 9-hydroxy-9H-xanthene-3-carboxylic acid (5) in the filtrate. The reaction solution was kept stirring at ambient temperature for 3 h. Then, the EtOH in the reaction solution was removed by rotary evaporation, and producing brown precipitate. After filtration, the precipitate was washed with distill water and dried, yielding XAA-295 (6). ESI-MS (negative) m/z:294 [M−H]− ; 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO) δ 9.12 (d, J =8.7 Hz, 1 H), 7.71 (dd, J = 8.0, 1.6 Hz, 1 H), 7.64 (d, J =1.7 Hz, 1 H), 7.50 (d, J =8.2 Hz, 1 H), 7.37 (dd, J = 11.2, 4.5 Hz, 2 H), 7.24 – 7.15 (m, 2 H), 6.41 (d, J =8.6 Hz, 1 H), 6.22 (dd, J = 6.2, 3.2 Hz, 2 H), 5.69 (dd, J = 8.3, 4.1 Hz, 1 H).

XAA-309. 2-(9-acrylamido-9H-xanthen-4-yl) acetic acid (12). Intermediate, xanthenone-4-acetic acids (10) was synthesized by referring to a previous work [29]. Sodium salts of 2-iodobenzoic acid (7, 35.1 g) and 2-methylphenol (8, 22.0 g) were mixed and suspended in anhydrous dioxane (300 mL), followed by addition of CuCl (1. 3 g) and tris[2-(2-methoxyethoxy) ethyl] amine (TDA-1) (4.2 g), and then the mixture was refluxed at 80 °C under the protection of N2 for 8 h. After removing the solvent by rotary evaporation, the residue was diluted with water and filtered. Then, HCl was used to acidify the filtrate, and producing precipitate which was collected by filtration, and washed with distill water, and dried to give intermediate, 2-(2-(carboxymethyl) phenoxy) benzoic acid (9). The intermediate (9, 2.9 g) was dissolved in H2SO4 (80 %, 100 mL) and refluxed at 80 °C under stirring for 3 h. When cooled to ambient temperature, the reaction mixture was poured over 300 mL of ice water yielding gray precipitate. After filtration, the gray precipitate was washed with water, and dried to obtain expected product as gray solid, xanthenone-4-acetic acids (10). Then, the intermediate (10, 1.4 g) was dissolved in 50 mL EtOH, followed by addition of sodium (0.6 g) and activated Zn powder (0.1 g). After refluxing for 24 h, the reaction solution was cooled to ambient temperature, and then HCl (6 mol/L) was used to acidify the reaction solution. Then, the formed precipitate was removed by filtration. Next, excess AA was added to react with 2-(9-hydroxy-9H-xanthen-4-yl) acetic acid (11) in the filtrate for 3 h. Then, the EtOH in the reaction solution was removed by rotary evaporation, and producing gray precipitate which was collected washed with distill water and dried, yielding XAA-309 (12). ESI-MS (negative) m/z:308 [M−H]−; 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO) δ 9.03 (d, J =8.8 Hz, 1 H), 7.40 – 7.32 (m, 2 H), 7.28 (t, J =7.4 Hz, 2 H), 7.19 – 7.06 (m, 3 H), 6.37 (d, J =8.7 Hz, 1 H), 6.23 (d, J =6.2 Hz, 2 H), 5.67 (t, J =6.2 Hz, 1 H), 3.74 (s, 2 H).

Active ester method was adopted to couple these two haptens to KLH and OVA, as immunogens and coating antigens, respectively. Brieﬂy, each hapten (0.1 mmol) was dissolved in 0.5 mL DMF followed by the addition of NHS (0.12 mmol) and DCC (0.12 mmol), and stirred at 4 °C for 12 h. Next, the mixture was dropwise added to 10 mL protein (25 mg KLH or 45 mg OVA) solution (prepared by PBS, 0.01 M, pH 8.0), respectively, and kept at 4 °C for 12 h with stirring. Then, unreacted haptens, and the by-product of conjugation reaction were removed by dialysis via semipermeable membrane against PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4) for 3 days. The resulting conjugation solution was kept under frozen storage (−20 °C) before use. The obtained hapten-carrier proteins were identified by UV–vis absorption spectrometry.

Animal treatments were in compliance with guidelines of the Chinese Association for Laboratory Animal Sciences. Each immunogen immunized two New Zealand white rabbit (10-wk-old, 1.5–2.0 kg) which were provided by the Guangdong Medical Laboratory Animal Center. The immunogen dose and immunization procedure were identical with those adopted in previous work [30]. Indirect competitive ELISA (ic-ELISA) was used to identify all the serum samples for their binding ability to XAA, as described in detail in Supplementary materials.

In brief, 21.6 mg of p-phenylenediamine and 29.4 mg of glutamic acid were dissolved in distill water and heated at 180 °C for 12 h in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave. Then, the resulting solution was dialyzed against distill water for 6 h with a semipermeable membrane (MWCO 1000 Da) to remove the unreacted molecules. Next, the solution was freeze-dried and then redissolved with distill water to make a CDs solution of 10 mg/mL. Finally, the synthesized CDs were stored at 4 °C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures of the IFE-based ﬂuorescent immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: Black opaque microplates were coated with 0.5 mg/L of coating antigens in carbonate buﬀ ;er (pH 9.6, 0.05 M), 100 μL/well, at 37 °C for 12 h. The plate was then rinsed with distill water twice, and blocked with 1% BSA in distill water (120 μL/well) at 37 °C for 3 h. After discarding the blocking solution, the plate was dried at 37 °C for 1 h. Treated sample solution or AA standard solution (50 μL/well) was added to the plates, followed by the addition of diluted pAb (in PBS, pH 7.4, 0.02 M, 50 μL/well). The plate was incubated at 37 °C for 40 min, followed by a washing step with distill water (five times). Next, 100 μL of ALP-labeled secondary antibody (1:2000 dilution in 0.01 M Tris−HCl, pH 9.0) was added to the wells and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C, followed by a washing step with distill water (five times). Then, pNPP solution (100 μL/well) was added to the plate and incubated for 30 min. Next, 50 μL of CDs solution (10 μg/mL) was added into each well and the ﬂuorescence intensity was recorded using a microplate reader (Spectra Max i3x, Molecular Devices, USA).

Calibration curve was constructed by plotting the ﬂuorescence intensity against the logarithm of AA concentrations. A four-parameter logistic equation was applied to produce the sigmoidal curve:
Y =
A − B
1 +
X C
D
+ B
where A and B were the maximal and minimal fluorescence intensity, respectively, C is IC50 value which is the concentration of AA that inhibited 50 % of coating antigen- antibody binding, D is the slope of the linear region in sigmoidal curve, and X is the concentration of AA.

Drinking water. Drinking water sample was directly subjected to the following derivatization procedure without further pretreatment. Sample of 0.6 mL, 0.4 mL of 9-xanthydrol (4 mg/mL) and 0.1 mL of HCl (0.5 mol/L) were mixed and hold at ambient temperature for 30 min. Then, 0.1 mL of NaOH (0.5 mol/L) was added to terminate the derivatization reaction. The derivatization reaction solution was then diluted 5-fold with distill water, and 50 μL of the diluted solution was assayed by the fluorescence immunoassay.

Potato chips and Cookie. Samples were purchased from a local supermarket. Smashed sample of 5.0 g and 20.0 mL of water were homogenized for 5 min, followed by centrifugation for 10 min at 8000 rpm in 50 mL centrifuge tubes. After a defatting step by 20 mL of n-hexane, sample extract was diluted 50-fold with distill water to resist the sample matrix effects. Then, the diluted cookies sample extract was subjected to the same derivatization and assay procedures as described above.

The fluorescence immunoassay was validated by the HPLC-MS/MS developed in our previous work [16]. A drinking water sample were spiked with AA standard at concentrations of 0, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 μg/L. The spiked drinking water samples were filtered through the microporous membrane (0.2 μm) and then analyzed by HPLC-MS/MS. A cookies sample and a potato chips sample were spiked with AA standard at concentrations of 0, 20, 50, and 100 μg/kg. The spiked cookies sample, and a potato chips samples (5 g) were extracted with 20 mL distill water and subjected to ultrasonication for 1 min, followed by a defatting step with 20 mL hexane, and then a centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Next, the extract supernatant was further cleaned by solid phase extraction on a strata-X-C cartridge (Phenomenex Inc., Torrance, CA, USA), and analyzed by HPLC-MS/MS. The analysis parameters of HPLC-MS/MS were presented in Supplementary materials. The results of HPLC-MS/MS were then compared with the results of the fluorescence immunoassay to evaluate the accuracy and reliability of the newly developed immunoassay.

Xanthydrol has been demonstrated to be an efficient derivatization which could convert AA into XAA (Mwt: 251 Da), in 30 min at ambient temperature [20,31]. As comparison, the derivatization reaction between AA and MBA involves heating (>50 ℃) and relative long time (>1 h). Thus, derivatization of AA using 9-xanthydrol would be advantageous over using MBA in terms of rapidity and facility, which better fits with the simplicity and rapidity concept of immunoassay. Similar to AA-3-MBA or AA-4-MBA, XAA also exhibits significantly improved size and aromaticity compared with AA which are beneficial factors contributing to T cell receptor and production of high quality anti-XAA antibody [32,33]. To produce highly specific antibody against XAA, the AA moiety not the aromatic rings in XAA should be the key epitope recognized by antibody. Therefore, we introduced a carboxyl group and a linker ending with a carboxyl group in C-2 and C-3 position of the aromatic ring moiety in XAA to give two haptens, XAA-295 and XAA-309, respectively. This design ensured the AA moiety of artificial antigens were in a site distal from carrier protein for the highly efficient interaction with animal immune system. Fig. 1B presents the synthetic scheme of haptens. ESI-MS and NMR spectra demonstrate the success in synthesis of hapten XAA-295 and XAA-309 (Figs. S1∼S4). Obvious blue shift in the characteristic protein peak of KLH at 280 nm after conjugating with the haptens indicated the successful preparation of immunogen (Fig. S5). After animal immunization, the performance of obtained antisera were evaluated by indirect competitive ELISA (ic-ELISA). As shown in Table 1 , four antibodies named after their corresponding haptens all exhibited a certain binding ability with XAA, especially Anti-XAA-309#1 showed the highest affinity to XAA, the ic-ELISA inhibition reached to 96.8 % in the presence of 100 μg/L XAA under heterologous coating format (XAA-295-OVA as coating antigen). Finally, Anti-XAA-309#1 and XAA-295-OVA was selected as the antibody and coating antigen combination to develop immunoassay for detection of AA using 9-xanthydrol derivatization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the inner filter effect-based fluorescence sensing of ALP
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of as-prepared CDs was characterized by TEM. Fig. 2 A showed that the CDs were well-dispersed and had a narrow size distribution (diameter 1.5–3.5 nm) with an average diameter of ∼2.4 nm. High-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2B) revealed that lattice fringes of CDs (0.21 nm) were matched well with the (100) facet of sp2graphitic carbon. The full range XPS analysis (Fig. S6A) of the as-prepared CDs sample shows three peaks at 284.5, 399.9, and 531.5 eV, which are attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the C1s peaks at 284.5, 285.0, 286.5, and 287.9 eV shown in Fig. S6B can be assigned to CC, CN, CO, CO, respectively [34]. The N 1s band (Fig. S6C) exhibits two peaks at 400.0 and 401.2 eV, respectively, which correspond to NC and NH groups [35]. FT-IR spectroscopy was also employed to characterize CDs. As depicted in Fig. 2C, the absorption peaks at 3425 cm−1, 1581 cm−1are attributed to the stretching vibrations and bending vibration of NH, respectively [36,37]. The absorption peak at 1740 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO [38]. The strong absorption peak at 1402 cm−1 indicates the presence of stretching vibration of CN [39]. These results are consistent with those of XPS. The solution of CDs in water emits strong green photoluminescence under UV light (365 nm). Fluorescence spectra of CDs displayed that its optimal excitation and emission wavelength were at 415 nm and 512 nm, respectively (Fig. 2D).

PNPP is a typical ALP substrate which can be catalytically hydrolyzed into p-nitrophenol (PNP). As shown in Fig. 2D, PNPP had a maximum absorption at 310 nm and could not influence both the excitation and emission of CDs, while the UV absorption spectrum of PNP overlapped the excitation spectrum of CDs. Fig. 3 A presented the change in UV absorption spectrum of the ALP reaction systems with various ALP concentrations. With the growth of ALP concentration, the absorption peak of PNPP at 310 nm gradually decreased, while the absorption peak of the released PNP at 405 nm increased, and the absorption toward the excitation light of the CDs by the released PNP steadily increased. As reflected in the fluorescence spectra, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched gradually with the growth in ALP concentration (Fig. 3B). The reduction in fluorescence intensity (F0 − F) exhibited a good linear relationship with ALP concentration in the range of 0.05 ∼ 10 U/L (Fig. 3C, Y = 7.24X + 0.393, R2 = 0.988). As presented in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence lifetime was not obviously change with the addition of PNP (11.42 ns) comparing with the free CDs (11.47 ns), indicating that no energy transfer or electron transfer occurred toward PNP-induced fluorescence quenching [40]. Additionally, the increase in temperature from 20 to 60 °C could not influence the quenching effect toward CDs (Fig. S7), further demonstrating that fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect did not appear in this system [41]. The above results suggested that the IFE process between CDs and PNP was dominant for the FL quenching mechanism. Therefore, sensitive fluorescence assay of ALP concentration at 0.03 U/L (S/N = 3) was attained by translating variations in the absorbance of PNP into exponential changes in the fluorescence of CDs. This IFE-based ALP sensing system exhibits promising potential to be extended into a high sensitivity ﬂuorescence immunoassay.

The IFE-based fluorescent immunoassay was developed by combining the IFE-based ALP sensing system with ALP-based ELISA platform. To achieve optimal performance of the ﬂuorescence immunoassay, checkerboard titration was used to optimize the coating antigens and pAb concentrations. It was displayed in Table S1 that the best assay sensitivity was attained when the coating concentration for coating antigen is at 0.5 mg/L, and the working concentration for anti-XAA pAb is 625 μg/L (diluting the anti-XAA pAb stocking solution 8000-fold in PBS). Since we had developed a traditional colorimetric ELISA using the same antibody-coating antigen combination (Fig. S7), optimal working buffer for antibody-antigen reaction and 9-xanthydrol derivatization conditions for fluorescent immunoassay were identical with those applied in ELISA.

Under the optimal conditions, serial concentrations of AA standards were derivatized into XAA according to the derivatization procedure described in Section 2.5, and then used as the competitive hapten in the fluorescent immunoassay to construct calibration curve. Lower concentration of AA means less XAA molecules would compete with coating antigens to capture anti-XAA antibody, and more coating antigen-antibody-ALP complex would form in 96-well plate, thus more PNP would release and the photoluminescence of CDs would be quenched to a larger extent. Contrary to typical competitive immunoassay, the concentration of target (AA) exhibits a positive correlation with fluorescence intensity in this proposed fluorescence immunoassay (Fig. 4 A). As displayed Fig. 4B, the ﬂuorescence exhibited a linear growth when the AA concentration increased in the range of 0.21∼6.48 μg/L, with a IC50 of 1.19 μg/L. The detection limit (LOD) was 0.16 ng μg/L (IC10), which was ∼50 times lower than that of conventional colorimetric ic-ELISA (LOD: 7.9 μg/L, Fig. S11). Such extraordinarily high sensitivity arises from its two distinctive features: (i) comparing with traditional colorimetric ic-ELISA, this fluorescence immunoassay possess fluorescence responds to AA, providing switchable fluorescence signal in high sensitivity; (ii) this fluorescence immunoassay is based on a “signal-on” mode, thus having lower background signal and higher sensitivity for quantification of AA [42,43].

The selectivity of immunoassay mainly depends on the specificity of antibody against target. Cross-reactivity (CR) test indicates the anti-XAA pAb showed negligible CR to 9-xanthydrol, since even 1000 mg/L of 9-xanthydrol induce no obvious variation in signal of the competitive ELISA, which ensure non-necessity in removing unreacted 9-xanthydrol after the derivatization step. For xanthyl derivatives of two AA analogs, XMC and XEC, the CR to them were less than 5%, which suggest lower or negligible CR would be observed for those compounds having greater difference with XAA in structure. Therefore, the anti-XAA pAb has good specificity toward XAA, implying that the proposed fluorescence immunoassay is highly specific to AA.

To investigate its accuracy, this fluorescence immunoassay was used to analyze AA in drinking water, cookies, and potato chips samples via the standard addition method. The AA-free drinking water sample (determined by HPLC-MS/MS) was spiked with three level of AA standard (0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 μg/L). Cookies and potato chips are typical foods prone to be contaminated with AA at a level over 100 μg/kg [44]. Meanwhile, these food samples are of complex matrix, an additional 50-fold dilution to the sample extract was found essential to eliminate the matrix effect. Thus, cookies sample and potato chips sample were spiked with relatively high level of AA standard (20, 50, 100 μg/kg). As presented in Table 2 , the recoveries were in the range of 82.3–93.5 % for drinking water, 81.0–105.6 % for cookies, and 88.7–103.2 % for potato chips. And the obtained results exhibited a good agreement with that of HPLC-MS/MS, implying the potential practicality of the fluorescence immunoassay for determination of AA in real samples.

In summary, by taking advantage of the high efficiency and simplicity of the 9-xanthydrol derivatization of AA as well as the high sensitivity of IFE-based fluorescence assay, we have produced haptens, artificial antigens and specific antibodies against xanthyl derivative of AA (XAA) as recognition reagents, and synthesized a green-emission CDs as a ﬂuorescent probe for ALP sensing, thus developing a highly sensitive IFE-based fluorescence immunoassay for detection of AA in foods. Compared with previous immunoassays for AA which require a relatively complex sample derivatization by MBA, this proposed fluorescence immunoassay could detect AA using a much simpler sample derivatization by 9-xanthydrol. Meanwhile, by converting the absorbance of PNP into highly sensitive fluorescent signal via IFE strategy, as well as transforming the typical “signal-off” mode of competitive immunoassay into a “signal-on” mode, this fluorescence immunoassay displayed excellent sensitivity to AA which meet the requirement for screening the AA in drinking water. In conclusion, this fluorescence immunoassay provides a potent strategy for AA detection.

Lin Luo: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Software, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Bao-Zhu Jia: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Software, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Xiao-Qun Wei: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Software, Validation, Writing - original draft. Zhi-Li Xiao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Software, Validation, Writing - original draft. Hong Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Yuan-Ming Sun: Methodology, Project administration, Supervision. Yu-Dong Shen: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Hong-Tao Lei: Formal analysis, Methodology. Zhen-Lin Xu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31822039, 31801621), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1602903), the Special Support Program of Guangdong Province, China (2019TX05N052) and the Key Project of Guangdong Provincial High School, China (2019KJDXM002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129561.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lin Luo received his Ph.D. degree in food science from South China Agricultural University. Currently, he is an associated professor in South China Agricultural University. His current research interests focus on development of functional nanomaterials for immunoassay and biosensors.

Baozhu Jia received her Master degree in food engineering from South China Agricultural University. Now, she is a lecturer at College of Biology and Food Engineering, Guangdong University of Education. Her research interests focus on the rapid detection technology for small molecule hazard factors in foods

Xiaoqun Wei received his Ph.D. degree in materials science and engineering from Sun Yat-sen University. Currently, he is an associated professor at South China Agricultural University. His main research direction is the rapid detection technology for small molecule hazard factors in foods

Zhili Xiao received her Ph.D. degree in food science from South China Agricultural University. Currently, she is an associated professor at South China Agricultural University. Her main research direction is the hapten design and synthesis and immunoassays for small molecule hazard factors in foods

Hong Wang received her Ph.D. degree in food science from South China University of Technology. Currently, she is a professor at South China Agricultural University. Her main research direction is antibody engineering and immunoassays for small molecule hazard factors in foods

Yuan-ming Sun received his Ph.D. degree in food science from South China Agricultural University. Currently, he is a professor at South China Agricultural University. His main research direction is the rapid detection technology for small molecule hazard factors in foods.

Yu-dong Shen received her Ph.D. degree in organic chemistry from Sun Yat-sen University. Currently, he is a professor at South China Agricultural University. His main research direction is the hapten design and synthesis and immunoassays for small molecule hazard factors in foods.

Hongtao Lei received his Ph.D. degree in food science from South China Agricultural University. Currently, he is a professor at South China Agricultural University. His main research direction is the rapid detection technology for small molecule hazard factors in foods.

Zhen-lin Xu received his Ph.D in Food Science from South China Agricultural Unviersity in 2010. He worked as a post-doctoral fellow at Chinese Academy of Sciences for 2 years. Currently, he is a professor in South China Agricultural University. His research interests include the production of antibodies against small molecules and the application of nanomaterials for immuno-sensors.
